through 8. A single box curriculum was required, an incorporated ESL component was mandated, and the

unfortunately weren't even on the state approved list. We were told that the district had viewed
reviewed multiple options, but we were never informed of what they were. The LCAT members invested,
some some LCAT members invested a lot of time and effort attending these meetings and doing
research.

[49:00–49:30]
And we were only given 10 minutes to review the final two options at that meeting. Despite the
community viewing period being short and not well publicized, I know several people who went and
viewed the curriculum and shared concerns with the district regarding the curriculum choices.
Unfortunately, I'm left deeply disappointed in how the process was handled and ultimately in the
curriculum that was selected. My main concerns regarding HMH into reading as our curriculum choice
are that it is not strongly aligned with the principles of the science of reading, and it still
incorporates elements of balanced literacy, which, as some of us know, has contributed to the wide

[49:30–50:00]
Has contributed to the widespread reading failure that is now seen across the United States. It does
not prioritize foundational skills as effectively as other curricula that was recommended by Texas
Reads. It is not a true knowledge building curriculum, despite research showing that knowledge
building is key to reading success. And I have yet to find external studies not conducted or
commissioned by HMH that show the program is effective or increases district literacy rates. I
joined the LCAT with a spirit of collaboration, hopeful that our work would drive real meaningful
change to improve student reading outcomes.

[01:06:00–01:06:30]
Thank you. Our next speaker is Kelly Bleaker. Recent data shows that uncertified teachers leave at a
rate three times higher than certified ones. I was once a certified teacher, uncertified teacher.
Teaching is hard. Teaching with no classroom experience and minimal training is overwhelmingly hard.
Any success I had didn't come from district feedback or curriculum or Teach for America. It came
from the support of veteran staff at my school who counted.

[01:06:30–01:07:00]
Who counseled me through missteps, shared best practices, knew the specific needs of my students,
and taught by example how to meet them. In light of the mass exodus of veteran teachers and admins
under your leadership, I ask you who will support HISD's 2,000 uncertified teachers to ensure
success and stability for our students? New teachers do not need error ridden slides and AI
generated packets. They need true mentorship. Not a supervisor looming like a sweatshop foreman
accounting for every minute not devoted to test prep. No trust, no bond.
